refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the system is available for productive operation to total scheduled operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts customer satisfaction and mission success. Refining it requires explicit definition of: (1) the measurement boundary (what constitutes 'available'), (2) the evaluation period (e.g., 30 days, 90 days, 1 year), (3) exclusions (planned maintenance windows, customer-initiated pauses), and (4) the role of safety-driven shutdowns. High safety criticality demands that availability not be achieved at the expense of safety; therefore, safety-driven unavailability is acceptable and must be distinguished from failure-driven unavailability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, failure events, maintenance records) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Unplanned Downtime − Safety Shutdowns) / Total Time. Compare against the 0.97 threshold. Conduct root-cause analysis on any unplanned downtime events to identify systemic failure modes.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall detect and log all unplanned downtime events (failures, faults, anomalies) with timestamp and root cause.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall implement predictive and preventive maintenance strategies to minimize unplanned downtime.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time availability status visibility to the Site Manager and Maintenance team.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall isolate and recover from transient faults without loss of availability.
FR-NGHE-004.5: The NGHE solution shall distinguish between safety-driven unavailability (acceptable) and failure-driven unavailability (unacceptable) in availability calculations.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a centralized availability monitoring and analytics engine that continuously tracks system state, detects failures, logs events, and calculates availability metrics in real-time; couple with predictive maintenance algorithms and automated fault recovery mechanisms.

DP-B: Distribute availability monitoring across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules, each reporting uptime/downtime to a lightweight aggregator; rely on module-level health checks and manual maintenance scheduling.

DP-C: Implement a redundant architecture with hot-standby components (dual vehicles, dual teleoperation links, dual safety controllers) to mask failures and maintain availability without explicit monitoring.

DP-D: Implement a hybrid approach combining centralized availability analytics (for KPI tracking and root-cause analysis) with distributed health monitoring (at Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services levels) and selective redundancy for critical paths.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a hybrid availability assurance architecture consisting of: (1) a centralized Availability Analytics Engine that aggregates telemetry from all subsystems, calculates real-time availability metrics, performs root-cause analysis, and generates alerts; (2) distributed health monitoring at the Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services levels, each reporting state and fault events via standardized interfaces; (3) selective redundancy and fault isolation for critical failure modes (e.g., dual safety controllers, redundant teleoperation links); (4) automated fault recovery and graceful degradation logic to minimize downtime; (5) predictive maintenance scheduling based on component wear models and historical failure data.

potential_sources: Coupling between Availability Analytics Engine and Vehicle health monitoring: mitigated by standardized Telemetry interface.
Coupling between Availability Analytics Engine and Teleoperation health monitoring: mitigated by standardized Telemetry interface.
Coupling between Availability Analytics Engine and Safety Services: mitigated by SafetyAlert interface; safety-driven shutdowns are logged as acceptable unavailability.
Coupling between fault recovery logic and Vehicle control: mitigated by clear ownership boundaries and Tasking interface.
Coupling between predictive maintenance and maintenance scheduling: mitigated by clear data flow and human-in-the-loop decision-making.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D exhibits low coupling because it leverages existing standardized interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) and maintains clear separation of concerns. The centralized analytics engine is a consumer of telemetry, not a controller of subsystems. Fault recovery is triggered by analytics but executed by subsystem-level logic. This design respects module boundaries and avoids diffusion.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: All subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) can emit standardized Telemetry at sufficient granularity and frequency to enable real-time availability calculation.
Assumption: Predictive maintenance algorithms can be trained on historical failure data; if data is sparse or unavailable, predictive capability may be limited.
Risk: If Telemetry interface is unreliable or has high latency, availability metrics may be inaccurate or delayed, leading to poor decision-making.
Risk: Selective redundancy adds cost and complexity; over-provisioning may be uneconomical, while under-provisioning may not achieve 0.97 target.
Risk: Automated fault recovery logic may mask underlying systemic issues, delaying root-cause resolution.
Risk: High safety criticality demands that fault recovery logic never compromises safety; this requires rigorous validation and may limit automation.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly define: (1) the Telemetry schema and frequency requirements for each subsystem; (2) the fault recovery decision tree and safety constraints; (3) the predictive maintenance algorithm and training data requirements; (4) the redundancy strategy (which components, which failure modes); (5) the roles and responsibilities of the Availability Analytics Engine vs. subsystem-level health monitors. Consider a phased implementation: Phase 1 (MVP) focuses on centralized monitoring and root-cause analysis; Phase 2 adds predictive maintenance; Phase 3 adds selective redundancy.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system integration boundary that owns the end-to-end availability assurance responsibility. It contains or orchestrates the Availability Analytics Engine, integrates health monitoring from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, and implements the overall fault recovery and predictive maintenance strategy. The NGHE Solution module is responsible for calculating and reporting the 0.97 availability KPI to stakeholders.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives health status, operational metrics, fault events, and performance data from Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules. Used by Availability Analytics Engine to calculate availability metrics and detect anomalies.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Sends maintenance scheduling commands and fault recovery directives to Vehicle and Teleoperation modules. Used to trigger automated recovery actions and coordinate maintenance windows.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety-critical alerts and shutdown signals from Safety Services. Used to distinguish safety-driven unavailability (acceptable) from failure-driven unavailability (unacceptable) in availability calculations.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Provides communication backbone for all telemetry, tasking, and safety alert messages. Critical for real-time availability monitoring and fault recovery.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level owner of availability assurance. It integrates all subsystems and is responsible for end-to-end KPI achievement. The Availability Analytics Engine, fault recovery orchestration, and predictive maintenance scheduling are all logically contained within NGHE Solution. Minor diffusion risk: some fault recovery logic may reside in Vehicle or Teleoperation modules (e.g., automatic failover), but this is acceptable as long as NGHE Solution coordinates and monitors.

Vehicle module owns vehicle-level health monitoring and fault recovery (e.g., engine diagnostics, drivetrain redundancy), but does not own the system-level availability analytics or cross-subsystem coordination. Assigning availability KPI ownership to Vehicle alone would diffuse responsibility and create coupling with Teleoperation and Safety Services.

Teleoperation module owns teleoperation-link health and redundancy, but does not own system-level availability analytics or coordination with Vehicle and Safety Services. Assigning availability KPI ownership to Teleoperation alone would be inappropriate and create diffusion.

Safety Services module owns safety monitoring and shutdown logic, but does not own system-level availability analytics or fault recovery. Safety-driven unavailability is a component of the availability calculation, but Safety Services should not own the overall KPI. Assigning availability KPI ownership to Safety Services would create inappropriate coupling and diffusion.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the appropriate owner of CR-NGHE-004 (Availability KPI) because: (1) it is the top-level system integration boundary; (2) it has visibility into all subsystems via standardized interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link); (3) it can implement centralized availability analytics without diffusing responsibility; (4) it can coordinate fault recovery and maintenance across subsystems; (5) it is accountable to stakeholders (Site Manager, Project Manager) for KPI achievement. Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) contribute to availability through their own health monitoring and fault recovery, but do not own the overall KPI.
containment_risks: Risk: If Telemetry interface is not sufficiently rich or timely, NGHE Solution may not have adequate visibility to calculate accurate availability metrics. Mitigation: Define Telemetry schema and frequency requirements in DP refinement.

Risk: If Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services modules do not emit standardized health events, NGHE Solution may struggle to aggregate and analyze. Mitigation: Enforce interface contracts and conduct integration testing.

Risk: If fault recovery logic is distributed across multiple modules without clear coordination, NGHE Solution may not be able to orchestrate effectively. Mitigation: Define fault recovery decision tree and ownership boundaries in DP refinement.

Risk: If Network Link is unreliable, telemetry and tasking messages may be lost, degrading availability monitoring and fault recovery. Mitigation: Implement message queuing, retry logic, and redundant communication paths.

Risk: Availability analytics engine may become a performance bottleneck if it processes high-frequency telemetry from all subsystems. Mitigation: Implement efficient data aggregation, filtering, and caching strategies.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine the NGHE Solution module boundary to explicitly define: (1) the Availability Analytics Engine as a contained sub-component with clear inputs (Telemetry, SafetyAlert) and outputs (availability metrics, alerts, maintenance recommendations); (2) the Fault Recovery Orchestrator as a contained sub-component that receives analytics outputs and issues Tasking commands; (3) the Predictive Maintenance Scheduler as a contained sub-component that analyzes historical data and generates maintenance plans; (4) the interfaces and data contracts between these sub-components and the subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). Consider whether the Availability Analytics Engine should be a separate module (e.g., 'Availability Services') if it grows in complexity or is reused across multiple systems.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid availability assurance architecture) directly addresses the refined FR (achieve ≥0.97 availability). The DP includes centralized monitoring (to measure availability), distributed health monitoring (to detect failures), fault recovery (to minimize downtime), and predictive maintenance (to prevent failures). The DP is logically sound and feasible. No decoupling or missing elements identified.

diffusion_risks: Minor diffusion risk: Fault recovery logic may be distributed across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services modules. Mitigation: NGHE Solution orchestrates via Tasking interface; subsystems execute autonomously but report status via Telemetry.

Minor diffusion risk: Predictive maintenance algorithms may require data from multiple subsystems. Mitigation: NGHE Solution aggregates Telemetry and performs analysis centrally; no algorithm diffusion.

Minor diffusion risk: Redundancy strategy (e.g., dual safety controllers) may require changes to Safety Services module. Mitigation: Redundancy is a design choice within Safety Services; NGHE Solution monitors and coordinates via SafetyAlert interface.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary for DP-D. The Availability Analytics Engine, Fault Recovery Orchestrator, and Predictive Maintenance Scheduler are logically contained within NGHE Solution. Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) contribute via standardized interfaces. No significant diffusion or coupling issues identified. The design respects module boundaries and leverages existing interfaces effectively.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of CR-NGHE-004 is feasible if: (1) Telemetry interface provides sufficient granularity and frequency; (2) operational data is collected over the agreed evaluation period; (3) availability calculation methodology is clearly defined and agreed with customer; (4) root-cause analysis tools are available. Recommend defining a detailed verification plan in the next level of decomposition.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation of CR-NGHE-004 to NGHE Solution is clear. The NGHE Solution module is responsible for: (1) implementing availability monitoring and analytics; (2) coordinating fault recovery across subsystems; (3) scheduling and executing maintenance; (4) reporting availability metrics to stakeholders. Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) are responsible for: (1) emitting health telemetry; (2) executing fault recovery directives; (3) supporting maintenance operations. Clear ownership boundaries and interface contracts are required.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D to define the Telemetry schema, frequency, and latency requirements for each subsystem. Specify which health metrics are mandatory vs. optional.
Refine DP-D to define the fault recovery decision tree, including safety constraints and automated vs. manual recovery actions.
Refine DP-D to define the predictive maintenance algorithm, training data requirements, and scheduling logic.
Refine DP-D to define the redundancy strategy, including which components are redundant, which failure modes are masked, and the cost/benefit tradeoff.
Define a detailed verification plan for CR-NGHE-004, including data collection methodology, availability calculation formula, evaluation period, and acceptance criteria.
Conduct a risk assessment on the Telemetry interface and Network Link to ensure they can support real-time availability monitoring without becoming bottlenecks or single points of failure.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-004 is a high-level KPI that requires further decomposition into more granular, actionable functional requirements. The refined FR identifies five sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-004.1 through FR-NGHE-004.5) that address specific aspects of availability assurance: failure detection and logging, predictive maintenance, status visibility, fault recovery, and safety-driven unavailability handling. Each sub-FR should be decomposed further to define specific DPs and IMs. The current level of decomposition is appropriate for a gate review, but further refinement is needed to drive detailed design and implementation.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall detect and log all unplanned downtime events (failures, faults, anomalies) with timestamp and root cause.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall implement predictive and preventive maintenance strategies to minimize unplanned downtime.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time availability status visibility to the Site Manager and Maintenance team.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall isolate and recover from transient faults without loss of availability.
FR-NGHE-004.5: The NGHE solution shall distinguish between safety-driven unavailability (acceptable) and failure-driven unavailability (unacceptable) in availability calculations.

assumptions: All subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) can emit standardized Telemetry at sufficient granularity and frequency.
Historical failure data is available or can be collected to train predictive maintenance algorithms.
The customer-defined availability target is 0.97 (97%) or will be specified during requirements refinement.
The evaluation period for availability measurement is agreed with the customer (e.g., 30 days, 90 days, 1 year).
Safety-driven unavailability (e.g., safety shutdowns) is acceptable and will be excluded from failure-driven unavailability calculations.
The NGHE Solution module has sufficient computational resources to run real-time availability analytics without becoming a bottleneck.
The Network Link is reliable enough to support real-time telemetry and tasking messages with acceptable latency.
open_risks: Risk: Telemetry interface may not provide sufficient granularity or frequency to enable accurate real-time availability calculation. Mitigation: Define Telemetry schema and frequency requirements in DP refinement; conduct interface design review.

Risk: Predictive maintenance algorithms may not be accurate if historical failure data is sparse or biased. Mitigation: Collect diverse failure data; validate algorithms on test data; implement human-in-the-loop review.
Risk: Fault recovery logic may mask underlying systemic issues, delaying root-cause resolution. Mitigation: Implement comprehensive logging and analytics; conduct regular root-cause analysis reviews.
Risk: Selective redundancy may not be sufficient to achieve 0.97 availability target. Mitigation: Conduct failure mode analysis; identify critical failure modes; implement appropriate redundancy.

Risk: Network Link may become a single point of failure for availability monitoring and fault recovery. Mitigation: Implement redundant communication paths; design fault recovery logic to work with degraded network connectivity.

Risk: High safety criticality may constrain fault recovery automation, limiting availability improvements. Mitigation: Conduct safety analysis; define safety constraints; implement human-in-the-loop recovery for safety-critical scenarios.
evidence_needed: Telemetry interface specification: schema, frequency, latency, reliability requirements.
Historical failure data: failure modes, failure rates, root causes, repair times.
Predictive maintenance algorithm design: algorithm selection, training data, validation methodology.
Fault recovery decision tree: failure modes, recovery actions, safety constraints, automation level.
Redundancy strategy: components, failure modes, cost/benefit analysis.
Network Link reliability analysis: latency, packet loss, availability, single points of failure.
Safety analysis: safety-critical failure modes, safety constraints, fault recovery limitations.
Availability calculation methodology: formula, exclusions, evaluation period, acceptance criteria.
Verification plan: data collection, analysis, reporting, stakeholder sign-off.
